proof_stages.label: Multiple-cause causal-inference problem and confounding
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates the goal of estimating intervention effects from observational data, explains why ordinary prediction fails under unobserved confounding, and isolates the special leverage available when there are many causes rather than one.

proof_stages.label: Deconfounder recipe via a cause-only factor model
proof_stages.summary: The proposed procedure models only the assigned causes with a probabilistic factor model, estimates a local latent variable for each unit, and treats that variable as a substitute confounder.

proof_stages.label: Model checking for the assigned-cause distribution
proof_stages.summary: The factor model must be checked to ensure that its local latent variable captures the distributional dependence among the causes, using Bayesian model criticism and predictive scores to choose among candidate factor models.

proof_stages.label: Outcome adjustment with substitute confounders
proof_stages.summary: After augmenting the data with estimated substitute confounders, standard causal adjustment methods such as regression adjustment, the g-formula, or backdoor adjustment are applied to estimate intervention contrasts.

proof_stages.label: Conditional-independence argument for capturing multi-cause confounders
proof_stages.summary: If the fitted factor model makes the assigned causes conditionally independent given its local latent variable, then any remaining unobserved multi-cause confounder would contradict that independence, while the theory also rules out accidentally capturing downstream mediators or colliders.

proof_stages.label: Identification assumptions, no-free-lunch tradeoffs, and extensions
proof_stages.summary: The identification theorem requires no unobserved single-cause confounders, a well-fitting factorization of the cause distribution, a pinpointing condition for the latent variable, and overlap, with resulting bias-variance and finite-sample issues motivating open problems.

proof_stages.label: Empirical validation through simulation
proof_stages.summary: Simulation studies, especially a GWAS-style height/SNP experiment with latent population groups, test whether factor-model quality predicts improved downstream causal-effect estimation.

examples.name: Movie-casting and James Bond confounding
examples.note: The running movie-revenue scenario, especially James Bondness making M, Q, and Moneypenny look valuable, generates the key intuition that shared latent structure among many causes can reveal multi-cause confounding.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker sets up the movie-casting variables, analyzes James Bondness as an unobserved multi-cause confounder, and reasons through how the deconfounder changes values for M, Q, and Sean Connery.
